analysis.headline: Moving from Software Engineer to Sr. Software Engineer is a 1-band jump in the same family, with the biggest shift in ownership and technical judgment rather than new tools.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Sr. Software Engineer JD calls for that the Software Engineer JD doesn't yet, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Performs hands-on extraction of DB resident logic into separate service-oriented storage
Follows established patterns and asks for guidance when blocked
right: Owns features end-to-end within a team; contributes meaningfully to system design discussions
Navigates ambiguous technical problems independently, proposing multiple solutions and weighing tradeoffs
growthAreas.synthesis: The Software Engineer JD is scoped to defined tasks within a sprint; the Sr. JD explicitly adds end-to-end feature ownership and independent tradeoff analysis. This is less about learning a new stack and more about being trusted to make the call when there isn't one obvious answer.

left: Works under close guidance from a senior engineer
right: Mentors junior engineers; begins influencing team technical decisions, standards, and code quality expectations
growthAreas.synthesis: This is the first formal mentoring expectation in the ladder. It doesn't require managing anyone, but it does mean junior engineers start looking to you for direction on standards and code quality, not just task help.

left: 5+ years development in C# with data management and storage design experience
3+ years SQL Server database development, writing SQL code and stored procedures
right: MVC frameworks (Angular1 preferred)
Experience building scalable cloud services
growthAreas.synthesis: These are additional hard skills the Sr. JD calls for that don't cluster into one theme, mostly broader framework and cloud-scaling exposure layered on top of the same core C#/SQL foundation.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily rhythm actually differs once you're navigating ambiguous problems instead of well-defined tickets.

left: Solves routine to moderate technical problems following established patterns
right: Self-directed with light oversight; drives delivery within a defined scope and flags risks proactively
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect fewer moments of 'how should I approach this' and more moments of deciding the approach yourself, then flagging risk to your manager rather than waiting to be asked about it.

left: Contributes within the immediate sprint team; builds relationships with peers
right: Partners with team members across functions to solve architectural and code-design challenges
dayToDay.synthesis: The daily collaboration radius widens slightly, from mostly your own sprint team to occasional cross-team design conversations.

mainTakeaways: The jump from Software Engineer to Sr. Software Engineer shows up most in Technical Depth & Architecture, specifically independent judgment on ambiguous problems rather than new technical skills.
The mentoring expectation is new and worth planning for early, since it's the first ladder step that asks you to influence others, not just deliver your own work.
Talk to your manager about which upcoming features would let you own something end-to-end, that's the fastest way to demonstrate you're already operating at this level.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to own the next feature end-to-end, including the design discussion, rather than picking up a pre-scoped ticket.
Offer to pair with or review code for a newer engineer on your team, even informally, before it's a formal expectation.
If your framework exposure is mostly backend, ask for a project that touches the front end to round out the "scalable cloud services" requirement.

analysis.headline: Moving from Sr. DevOps Engineer to DevOps Manager is a 2-band jump that trades hands-on infrastructure ownership for team leadership and cross-functional delivery.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the DevOps Manager JD calls for that the Sr. DevOps Engineer JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Mentors peers on DevOps best practices; influences tooling and process decisions across engineering teams
right: Leads and manages the DevOps team, ensuring effective collaboration and project delivery
Trains and mentors team members, especially within development and support teams
growthAreas.synthesis: The Sr. Engineer JD already has informal mentoring built in, so this isn't a cold start. What's new is formal accountability for the team's delivery and collaboration, not just their technical growth.

left: Partners with engineering teams without waiting to be asked
right: Collaborates with the development team to improve developer tooling and processes
Ensures effective communication and coordination within the team and with other departments
growthAreas.synthesis: The scope of collaboration shifts from technical partnership with engineers to representing the whole DevOps function to other departments, which requires a different kind of communication than a peer-to-peer technical conversation.

left: 3+ years experience as a DevOps Engineer, 2+ years using AWS
Detailed technical knowledge of database and operating system security
right: Proven experience in DevOps, system administration, or a related field
Documentation skills to thoroughly document processes and solutions
growthAreas.synthesis: The formal requirements actually converge quite a bit at this step, the Manager JD reads more like a leadership overlay on the same technical foundation rather than a new specialty.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from doing infrastructure work to running a team that does it.

left: Self-directed; drives improvements proactively
right: Leads and manages the DevOps team, ensuring effective collaboration and project delivery
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect less time in Terraform and CI/CD pipelines yourself, and more time unblocking your team's work on those same systems and reviewing their approach instead of building it yourself.

left: Guides and performs security activities such as risk assessments and vulnerability testing
right: Secures, maintains, and enhances platform environment, deployment pipelines and infrastructure
dayToDay.synthesis: Security and infrastructure ownership carries over conceptually, but day-to-day it becomes something you're accountable for through your team's work rather than something you personally execute.

mainTakeaways: The jump from Sr. DevOps Engineer to DevOps Manager shows up most in Leadership & People Management, this is a genuine move from doing the work to leading people who do it.
Day-to-day, the biggest shift is in Leadership & People Management too, expect meaningfully less hands-on infrastructure time.

Talk to your manager or the People Team about a stretch project leading a small initiative or informally managing a contractor, since formal people-management experience is the one thing this JD calls for that a strong IC track alone won't build.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to lead a specific cross-team initiative (like an on-call process redesign) so you get real experience coordinating people rather than just systems.
Volunteer to represent DevOps in a planning or roadmap conversation with another department instead of sending updates secondhand.

analysis.headline: Moving from Customer Support Manager to Director of Customer Support is a 1-band jump from managing one front-line team to owning the entire global support function.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Director JD calls for that the Manager JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Ensures strict adherence to customer service tier agreements (SLAs) and escalates critical issues as needed
right: Sets support strategy, staffing plans, and operational goals aligned with company growth and customer needs
Owns support-wide performance metrics (SLAs, CSAT, resolution time) and drives continuous improvement initiatives
growthAreas.synthesis: The Manager JD is about hitting SLA targets within an existing structure; the Director JD is about setting that structure and the goals themselves for the whole function, which is a genuinely different planning horizon.

left: Leads, manages, hires and trains offshore Tier 1 and Tier 2 support teams
right: Leads and develops multiple support managers and their teams, building a strong culture of accountability and customer focus
growthAreas.synthesis: This is the classic manager-of-managers step: instead of directly leading agents, you're developing the managers who lead them, which changes coaching from tactical ("handle this ticket this way") to structural ("how are you developing your team").

left: 5+ years of experience in customer support or technical support roles; management experience is a plus
right: 7+ years of experience in technical support leadership, including managing managers
Experience with support tooling, ticketing systems, and performance analytics
growthAreas.synthesis: These are additional experience thresholds the Director JD calls for that don't cluster into one theme, mostly tenure and analytics fluency layered on top of the same support-leadership foundation.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from running one team's queue to setting direction for the whole function.

left: Collaborates closely with the team and third-party leadership team to define strategy and manage tactical initiatives
right: Partners with Engineering, Product, and Customer Success leadership to resolve systemic issues and inform the product roadmap
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect more time in cross-functional meetings about systemic patterns (why does this keep happening) and less time reviewing individual escalated tickets.

left: Works alongside Tier 3 Support agents, engineering, and product teams to identify and resolve recurring issues
right: Oversees escalation management for major incidents, ensuring clear communication with customers and internal stakeholders
dayToDay.synthesis: Escalation involvement carries over, but the Director role adds direct external-facing communication during major incidents, which the Manager role doesn't call for.

mainTakeaways: The jump from Customer Support Manager to Director of Customer Support shows up most in Strategy & Planning, this is the step where you go from executing a plan to setting one.
Day-to-day, that same shift plays out as more roadmap-facing, cross-functional meetings and less individual ticket-level involvement.
This role also introduces manager-of-managers coaching for the first time, worth asking your manager how that's evaluated differently from direct people management.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to co-own next quarter's support staffing or SLA targets with your Director, rather than just executing against targets already set.
Look for an opportunity to coach another team lead or manager, even informally, since manager-of-managers experience is the clearest gap between these two levels.

analysis.headline: Moving from Sales Engineer to Sr. Sales Engineer is a 1-band jump that trades supporting deals for owning the most complex and strategic ones.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Sr. Sales Engineer JD calls for that the Sales Engineer JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Handles moderate to complex technical questions
Designs and presents technical solutions and network architectures tailored to prospect needs
right: Owns complex technical presales engagements; leads solution design, POCs, and enterprise-tier technical solutioning
Navigates highly technical, multi-integration scenarios; architects network solutions for enterprise customers
growthAreas.synthesis: The scope moves from handling moderately complex questions to owning the hardest, most integration-heavy enterprise deals end to end, this is depth of technical ownership, not a new skill set.

left: Technical credibility bridge between Sales and customers
right: Mentors and supports the development of junior sales engineers
Shapes SE methodology; advocates for product roadmap based on field insights
growthAreas.synthesis: This is the first mentoring and methodology-shaping expectation in this track, similar to a technical-lead step rather than a management one, you're still individually contributing but others start learning from your approach.

left: 3+ years of experience in a sales engineering, solutions engineering, or technical pre-sales role
right: 5+ years of experience in a sales engineering, solutions engineering, or technical pre-sales role
Contributes to sales enablement materials and technical sales playbooks
growthAreas.synthesis: The tenure bar goes up, and there's a new expectation to help build the playbooks other SEs will use, which is a step toward institutional knowledge-building rather than just deal execution.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts once you're the escalation point instead of the one escalating.

left: Partners with Account Executives on technical discovery calls to understand prospect network environments
right: Serves as a technical advisor and escalation point for Account Executives across the sales organization
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect fewer deals where you're the primary technical contact throughout, and more moments where other SEs or AEs bring you in specifically because a deal has gotten technically difficult.

left: Responds to technical questions in RFPs, RFIs, and security questionnaires
right: Owns technical responses for complex RFPs, RFIs, and security questionnaires
dayToDay.synthesis: RFP involvement carries over, but ownership shifts from responding to specific sections to owning the complex, high-stakes ones in full.

mainTakeaways: The jump from Sales Engineer to Sr. Sales Engineer shows up most in Technical Depth & Architecture, specifically taking ownership of the hardest enterprise deals rather than learning new technology.
Day-to-day, that plays out as becoming the escalation point other SEs and AEs turn to, rather than being the primary contact on every deal yourself.
The mentoring and playbook-building expectations are new here and worth building intentionally, since they signal readiness for this step as much as deal complexity does.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to shadow or co-lead the next enterprise-tier deal with multi-integration requirements, rather than a standard demo cycle.
Offer to help build or update a piece of the sales enablement playbook, that's a low-risk way to start building the methodology-shaping track record this level expects.

analysis.headline: Moving from Product Manager to Senior Product Manager is a 1-band jump from owning a product domain to shaping strategy and enterprise relationships across it.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Senior PM JD calls for that the PM JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Serves as the owner and visionary for assigned components or products within ElevenOS, gathering and prioritizing requirements
right: Establishes a product vision and roadmap that supports and aligns with the overall product strategy and vision
growthAreas.synthesis: Both roles own vision at the component level, but the Senior PM JD explicitly ties that vision to the company-wide product strategy, meaning the planning horizon and the audience for that thinking both expand.

left: Establishes and maintains relationships with Customers and Partners relevant to this role
right: Serves as the chief sales engineer for assigned products as needed, demonstrating and selling the value of the ElevenOS platform
Establishes and maintains relationships with tech vendors and integration partners
growthAreas.synthesis: Customer-facing work carries over, but the Senior PM JD adds a sales-facing dimension (literally selling the platform's value) and vendor/integration-partner relationships, which is a broader external footprint than the PM level calls for.

left: 5+ years of experience with a complex product at an enterprise SaaS company
right: 5+ years as a product manager for a complex product at an enterprise SaaS company, having launched multiple new products
Participates in industry workgroups and associations, providing thought leadership on emerging trends
growthAreas.synthesis: The core experience bar is similar, but the Senior PM JD specifically calls for a track record of shipped new products, plus an external thought-leadership presence that the PM level doesn't ask for.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts once you're expected to represent the product externally, not just build it internally.

left: Makes thoughtful decisions on the fly, constantly clearing roadblocks to delivering value
right: Establishes a product vision and roadmap that supports and aligns with the overall product strategy and vision
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect more time zoomed out on where the roadmap is headed over multiple quarters, and comparatively less time in the weeds of clearing individual delivery roadblocks.

left: Establishes and maintains relationships with Customers and Partners relevant to this role
right: Serves as the chief sales engineer for assigned products as needed
dayToDay.synthesis: Some weeks will look like being pulled into live sales conversations to speak to product value directly, which isn't a standard expectation at the PM level.

mainTakeaways: The jump from Product Manager to Senior Product Manager shows up most in Strategy & Planning, moving from owning a component's vision to shaping company-wide product strategy.
Day-to-day, that plays out as more strategic, roadmap-level time and occasional direct involvement in sales conversations, both new at this level.
Industry thought leadership and a track record of shipping multiple new products are explicit requirements here, worth discussing with your manager which of those you can start building now.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to present your product's multi-quarter roadmap directly to leadership, rather than just your immediate team, to build comfort with that broader strategic framing.
Volunteer to join a sales call for your product when a technical or value-based question comes up, that's the fastest way to build the "chief sales engineer" muscle.

analysis.headline: Moving from Accounting Supervisor / Manager to Controller is a 1-band jump from running accounting operations to owning the company's financial controls end to end.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Controller JD calls for that the Accounting Supervisor / Manager JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Ensures compliance with accounting policies, procedures, and internal controls
right: Establishes and maintains strong internal controls and accounting policies
Leads external audit processes and manages relationships with auditors
growthAreas.synthesis: The Supervisor/Manager JD is about following and enforcing existing controls; the Controller JD is about designing those controls and personally owning the external audit relationship, a meaningfully bigger risk surface.

left: Supervises accounting staff responsible for accounts payable, accounts receivable, and general ledger activities
Mentors and develops accounting team members
right: Manages and develops the accounting team
Partners with the VP of Finance on budgeting, forecasting, and financial strategy
growthAreas.synthesis: Team leadership carries over directly, what's new is a formal partnership with the VP of Finance on strategy, meaning your team's output starts feeding directly into company-level financial decisions.

left: 4+ years of accounting experience, including supervisory experience
right: 7+ years of progressive accounting experience, including leadership roles
CPA certification (preferred)
growthAreas.synthesis: These are additional experience and credential expectations that don't cluster into one theme, mainly tenure and CPA certification layered on top of the same accounting and leadership foundation.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from running close and compliance to owning the company's financial integrity.

left: Reviews and approves journal entries, reconciliations, and financial reports
right: Ensures accurate and timely monthly, quarterly, and annual financial close and reporting
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, review responsibility carries over but the accountability widens to the entire close and reporting cycle across every cadence, not just reviewing what's already been prepared.

left: Partners with the Controller and Finance leadership on process improvements and audit preparation
right: Leads external audit processes and manages relationships with auditors
dayToDay.synthesis: You go from supporting audit preparation to being the person auditors work with directly, a real shift in exposure and accountability during audit season specifically.

mainTakeaways: The jump from Accounting Supervisor / Manager to Controller shows up most in Process, Risk & Compliance, moving from following controls to designing and owning them.
Day-to-day, that same shift plays out as direct ownership of the external audit relationship, which is a new and highly visible responsibility at this level.
A CPA certification and 7+ years of progressive experience are explicit in the Controller JD, worth mapping out a timeline for those with your manager if you're targeting this move.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to take a larger role in the next external audit cycle, even just coordinating logistics with auditors, to build direct exposure before it's expected of you.
If you don't already hold a CPA, ask your manager whether Eleven supports exam costs or study time, since it's explicitly called out in the Controller JD.

analysis.headline: Moving from Director of Customer Success to VP of Customer Success is a 1-band jump from running the CS function to owning the entire customer success and support organization at an executive level.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the VP JD calls for that the Director JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Drives strategy for scaling CS; resolves systemic account issues
right: Sets company-wide CS vision; navigates competitive and market dynamics
Develops strategies and business plans with partners and executes against objectives and milestones
growthAreas.synthesis: Both roles involve strategy, but the VP JD explicitly ties it to market and competitive dynamics company-wide, plus formal partner business planning, a broader and more externally exposed kind of strategic work.

left: Serves as the primary point of contact for key accounts, building strong relationships, addressing client needs promptly
right: Conducts visits to current and prospective clients, creating connections at the VP or C-Level
Manages SLA agreements and reporting with the brands, and is available 24/7 for any service disruptions
growthAreas.synthesis: Key-account relationship ownership carries over, but the VP JD adds direct C-level relationship building and personal SLA accountability, which is a different level of external visibility and always-on responsibility.

left: 5+ years relevant experience, including managing teams
PMP certification or experience managing complex cross-functional projects
right: 7+ years of experience in business development, partner management, customer success, or product management
5+ years of successfully managing a team, with demonstrated ability managing remote teams
growthAreas.synthesis: These are additional experience thresholds that don't cluster into one theme, mainly a broader background across business development and partner management, not just customer success specifically.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts once you're accountable to the CEO and board, not just running the department.

left: Ensures efficient execution by bridging the gap between big-picture strategy and detailed implementation
right: Actively participates on Eleven's management team in support of broader strategic issues
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect regular participation in company-wide leadership conversations that go beyond CS specifically, rather than mostly translating strategy into your own team's execution.

left: Partners with stakeholders to analyze process challenges, assess risks, identify opportunities for efficiency and scalability
right: In coordination with the VP of Engineering, leads the effort to ensure Eleven is compliant with various regulations like GDPR
dayToDay.synthesis: Cross-functional partnership carries over, but the VP role adds direct co-ownership of company-wide compliance work with another VP, which the Director role doesn't call for.

mainTakeaways: The jump from Director to VP of Customer Success shows up most in Strategy & Planning, moving from department strategy to company-wide, market-facing strategy.
Day-to-day, that plays out as regular involvement in company-wide leadership and compliance conversations, not just CS-specific ones.
C-level relationship building and 24/7 SLA accountability are explicit and new at this level, worth discussing with your manager how that visibility is built up over time rather than all at once.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to represent CS in a company-wide strategy or leadership discussion outside your normal scope, to build comfort operating at that level before it's expected.
Look for an opportunity to build a relationship with a client contact at the VP or C-level directly, rather than through an account team intermediary.

analysis.headline: Moving from Sr. Software Engineer to Engineering Manager is a 2-band jump onto the management track, trading individual technical ownership for team accountability.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Engineering Manager JD calls for that the Sr. Software Engineer JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Mentors junior engineers; begins influencing team technical decisions, standards, and code quality expectations
right: Manages a team of 4-8 engineers; accountable for delivery, velocity, and growth
Hires, develops, and retains talent; represents the team to Product and leadership
growthAreas.synthesis: The Sr. Engineer JD already has informal mentoring; the Manager JD adds formal accountability for hiring, performance, and team delivery, this is the actual management step, not an extension of technical mentoring.

left: Identifies and incorporates security requirements in the software development lifecycle process
right: Guides the Engineering team's development to ensure procedures for secure software development practices and deployment processes are followed
growthAreas.synthesis: Security ownership shifts from doing it yourself to making sure your whole team does it consistently, which is a supervisory responsibility rather than a technical one.

left: Front-end development experience (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, asynchronous callbacks)
right: Experience shipping commercial software product in a small-team environment
Experience designing, testing, documenting, and debugging distributed systems
growthAreas.synthesis: These are additional hard and/or soft skills that don't cluster into one broader theme, mostly a broader systems view layered on top of the same engineering foundation.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from writing and reviewing code to running a team's delivery.

left: Self-directed with light oversight; drives delivery within a defined scope
right: Balances people, process, and technical tradeoffs; navigates performance issues, delivery risks, and sprint health simultaneously
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect regular 1:1s, performance conversations, and sprint health checks to take up real time that used to go toward your own coding work.

left: Partners with team members across functions to solve architectural and code-design challenges
right: Works with Product Management and Engineering leadership to prioritize sprints that balance roadmap features, tech debt, and bugs
dayToDay.synthesis: Cross-functional partnership carries over, but the conversation shifts from solving a specific technical problem together to negotiating what the team works on next.

mainTakeaways: The jump from Sr. Software Engineer to Engineering Manager shows up most in Leadership & People Management, this is a genuine track change, not a technical promotion.
Day-to-day, that plays out as significantly less hands-on coding time and much more people and delivery management.

Since this is a track change rather than a linear step, it's worth having an honest conversation with your manager about whether you want to manage people day to day, not just whether you're technically ready.

worthExploring.rec: Ask for a stretch project acting as informal lead for a small initiative or a new hire's onboarding, to get a real preview of people-management responsibilities before committing to the track.
Volunteer to own your team's security review process for a cycle, rather than just completing your own portion of it.

analysis.headline: Moving from Principal Engineer to Engineering Manager is a lateral track change from the top of the individual-contributor ladder to the management ladder, not a step up in seniority.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Engineering Manager JD calls for that the Principal Engineer JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Shapes engineering culture, hiring bar, and org-wide practices; trusted by the CTO and product leadership
right: Manages a team of 4-8 engineers; accountable for delivery, velocity, and growth
Runs hiring, 1:1s, and performance cycles independently
growthAreas.synthesis: Principal Engineer already shapes culture and hiring bar at an org level; Engineering Manager narrows that influence to a specific team but adds direct, formal accountability for their day-to-day performance and growth, a different kind of leadership than org-wide technical influence.

left: Defines solutions for the most complex and ambiguous technical problems; comfortable with incomplete information and long time horizons
right: Manages 1-2 product facing engineering teams, facilitating Scrum ceremony leadership and management of flow of work to developers
growthAreas.synthesis: These don't cluster into one theme, but the underlying shift is real: Principal Engineer optimizes for solving the hardest technical problems personally, Engineering Manager optimizes for a team consistently delivering good work, which are genuinely different skill sets.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from solving the hardest technical problems yourself to running a team's delivery process.

left: Fully autonomous; self-selects the highest-impact work and operates across boundaries with no prompting
right: Owns team outcomes; escalates strategic decisions to Director; runs hiring, 1:1s, and performance cycles independently
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect a shift from choosing your own highest-impact technical work to being responsible for a team's collective output, including people issues that don't have a clean technical answer.

mainTakeaways: This is a lateral move between two different ladders, not a promotion, worth being explicit with yourself and your manager about that framing.
Day-to-day, the biggest change is trading deep, autonomous technical problem-solving for team-level people management, a real shift in what a typical week looks like.

If you're considering this move, it's worth talking to a current Engineering Manager about how much they miss hands-on technical work, since that tradeoff is real and permanent while you're in the role.

worthExploring.rec: Shadow an Engineering Manager for a week of 1:1s and planning meetings before deciding this is the track you want, it's a meaningfully different day than a Principal Engineer's.

analysis.headline: Moving from Principal Architect to Engineering Manager is a lateral track change from owning platform architecture to owning a team's people and delivery.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Engineering Manager JD calls for that the Principal Architect JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Focuses on being a mentor and role model to entire team, makes time available to support team member learning
right: Manages a team of 4-8 engineers; accountable for delivery, velocity, and growth
Runs hiring, 1:1s, and performance cycles independently
growthAreas.synthesis: The Architect JD already includes mentoring and role-modeling, but Engineering Manager adds direct, formal accountability for a specific team's hiring, performance reviews, and delivery, real management authority rather than influence through expertise.

left: Ensures that technical designs are properly evaluated and advises teams on how to improve execution
right: Guides the Engineering team's development efforts towards successful project delivery
growthAreas.synthesis: These don't cluster into one theme, but reflect the same underlying shift: advising on technical execution across teams versus being personally accountable for one team's delivery outcomes.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from advising on architecture across teams to running one team's delivery.

left: Strategic autonomy; partners with CTO and executive team on platform vision and major investment decisions
right: Owns team outcomes; escalates strategic decisions to Director; runs hiring, 1:1s, and performance cycles independently
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect meetings with your own team about their work and growth rather than executive conversations about platform-wide investment decisions, a narrower but more hands-on people focus.

mainTakeaways: This is a lateral move onto the management ladder, not a step up from Principal Architect, worth framing it that way with your manager.
Day-to-day, the shift is from broad architectural influence across the company to direct ownership of one team's people and delivery.

If you value staying close to platform-level technical decisions, it's worth clarifying how much of that this specific Engineering Manager role would still involve versus a pure people-management scope.

worthExploring.rec: Talk to your manager about whether there's a hybrid path (like a tech lead or staff-plus role) if you want people leadership without fully leaving architecture work behind.

analysis.headline: Moving from Engineering Manager to Sr. Engineering Manager is a 1-band jump from running one team to owning multiple teams and their cross-team dependencies.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Sr. Engineering Manager JD calls for that the Engineering Manager JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Hires, develops, and retains talent; represents the team to Product and leadership
right: Significant autonomy over resourcing and delivery strategy; partners with Directors and PMs on roadmap planning
growthAreas.synthesis: Team-level hiring and development carries over, but the Sr. EM JD adds resourcing decisions across multiple teams, meaning you're now balancing competing needs between teams, not just developing one.

left: Works with Product Management and Engineering leadership to prioritize sprints that balance roadmap features, tech debt, and bugs
right: Resolves organizational friction and delivery conflicts; leads complex multi-team initiatives with competing priorities
growthAreas.synthesis: Sprint-level prioritization for one team becomes resolving conflicts and dependencies between multiple teams, a genuinely more complex coordination problem with more stakeholders in the room.

left: 3+ years of experience leading developers using, designing, and implementing user experiences utilizing: C#, HTTP APIs, SQL Server databases
right: Strong org-level influence; partners with Directors and VPs on roadmap, headcount, and technical strategy
growthAreas.synthesis: These are additional expectations that don't cluster into one theme, mainly a step up in scope from team-level technical involvement to org-level strategic input.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from running one team's delivery to coordinating several.

left: Owns team outcomes; escalates strategic decisions to Director; runs hiring, 1:1s, and performance cycles independently
right: Manages multiple teams; owns cross-team programs, dependencies, and resource allocation
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect more time in dependency and resourcing conversations between teams, and comparatively less time in the details of any single team's sprint.

mainTakeaways: The jump from Engineering Manager to Sr. Engineering Manager shows up most in Cross-Functional & Stakeholder Collaboration, this is fundamentally about coordinating across teams, not managing one better.
Day-to-day, that plays out as more time resolving dependency conflicts between teams and less time in any single team's sprint details.
Since this step is about managing complexity across teams, it's worth asking your Director for exposure to a cross-team initiative now, before it's a formal expectation.

worthExploring.rec: Volunteer to represent your team in a cross-team dependency or roadmap planning conversation, rather than having your Director handle it.
Ask your Director for visibility into how resourcing decisions get made across teams, since that's a new muscle at this level.

analysis.headline: Moving from DevOps Engineer to Sr. DevOps Engineer is a 1-band jump from handling infrastructure tasks under guidance to proactively driving infrastructure improvements.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Sr. DevOps Engineer JD calls for that the DevOps Engineer JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Limited application development in Python, Node, or TypeScript (troubleshoot a ticket, a few times a month)
right: Solves complex infrastructure challenges; proposes automation and tooling improvements to reduce manual overhead
growthAreas.synthesis: The DevOps Engineer JD scopes coding to occasional troubleshooting; the Sr. JD expects you to proactively propose and build automation, a shift from reactive fixes to designing improvements.

left: Contributes within the DevOps team; collaborates with developers on deployment readiness
right: Mentors peers on DevOps best practices; influences tooling and process decisions across engineering teams
growthAreas.synthesis: This is the first mentoring and cross-team influence expectation in this ladder, meaning your tooling opinions start shaping decisions beyond just your own work.

left: 3+ years of experience as a DevOps Engineer, 2+ years using AWS
right: Self-directed; drives improvements proactively; partners with engineering teams without waiting to be asked
growthAreas.synthesis: These don't cluster into one theme, but reflect a real shift from executing defined infrastructure tasks to identifying and driving improvements without being asked.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from following runbooks to proactively improving the systems those runbooks cover.

left: Handles routine infrastructure issues and incidents; follows runbooks and escalates novel failures appropriately
right: Designs and improves CI/CD pipelines; owns reliability for key services; leads infra improvements that reduce toil
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect less time following existing runbooks and more time redesigning the systems and pipelines those runbooks describe, so the toil doesn't recur.

mainTakeaways: The jump from DevOps Engineer to Sr. DevOps Engineer shows up most in Technical Depth & Architecture, moving from following established patterns to proactively redesigning them.
Day-to-day, that plays out as measurably less time on routine runbook-driven work and more time on improvements that reduce that routine work going forward.
The mentoring and cross-team influence pieces are new here, worth building intentionally since they're part of what distinguishes this level, not just technical depth alone.

worthExploring.rec: Pick one recurring manual task or incident type and propose an automation project to eliminate it, rather than waiting to be assigned one.
Share a tooling recommendation or lesson learned with another engineering team, even informally, to start building cross-team influence.

analysis.headline: Moving from Sr. SecOps Engineer to DevOps Manager is a cross-family, 2-band jump from deep security ownership to leading a DevOps and/or SecOps team.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the DevOps Manager JD calls for that the Sr. SecOps Engineer JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Advises engineering on secure design patterns; trains team on threat modeling and security best practices
Mentors teammates and supports cross-functional security needs
right: Leads and manages the DevOps team, ensuring effective collaboration and project delivery
Trains and mentors team members, especially within development and support teams
growthAreas.synthesis: The Sr. SecOps JD already includes training and mentoring; the Manager JD adds formal team leadership and delivery accountability, a real management step rather than an extension of the security specialty.

left: Designs and maintains a highly secure and scalable AWS-based infrastructure using security services like AWS WAF, IAM, and Inspector
right: Secures, maintains, and enhances platform environment, deployment pipelines and infrastructure
growthAreas.synthesis: The scope broadens from deep security-specific infrastructure work to general platform and deployment-pipeline ownership, meaning the role covers DevOps concerns beyond just security.

left: 10+ years in SecOps, DevOps, and/or security engineering roles with heavy AWS usage, including at least 5 years in SecOps
right: Proven experience in DevOps, system administration, or a related field
Agile and adaptable mindset to thrive in a small, fast-paced company
growthAreas.synthesis: These don't cluster into one theme, but the underlying shift is from being a security specialist to a generalist DevOps leader who happens to have deep security depth.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from hands-on security engineering to leading a team across both DevOps and security concerns.

left: Largely self-directed; defines security standards and tooling choices within their domain
right: Leads and manages the DevOps team, ensuring effective collaboration and project delivery
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect team coordination and delivery management to take up real time that previously went to hands-on security engineering and incident investigation.

mainTakeaways: This is a cross-family jump from a deep security specialty to a broader DevOps leadership role, worth confirming with your manager how much day-to-day security depth this specific manager role retains.
Day-to-day, the biggest shift is trading hands-on security engineering for team leadership across a wider DevOps scope.
Since the JD calls for DevOps and/or SecOps team leadership, ask specifically which team composition this role would inherit, that changes how much of your existing specialty carries over directly.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to lead a small cross-functional security initiative involving both DevOps and SecOps engineers, to build experience managing across both disciplines before this move.

analysis.headline: Moving from Sr. Network Engineer (Tier 3) to Customer Support Manager is a cross-family jump from deep technical escalation work to formally managing a front-line support team.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Customer Support Manager JD calls for that the Sr. Network Engineer JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Mentors engineering team; shapes technical knowledge base
right: Leads, manages, hires and trains offshore Tier 1 and Tier 2 support teams, providing guidance, performance monitoring, ongoing coaching and support
growthAreas.synthesis: Mentoring within the engineering team carries over in spirit, but the Manager JD adds formal hiring, performance monitoring, and coaching responsibility for a whole front-line team, a real management step.

left: Acts in a key capacity for product validation, go/no-go decisions and pilots
right: Ensures strict adherence to customer service tier agreements (SLAs) and escalates critical issues as needed
Creates and implements team KPIs and establishes comprehensive reporting frameworks
growthAreas.synthesis: Product-level judgment calls shift to operational SLA and KPI ownership for a team, a different kind of decision-making focused on team performance rather than technical validation.

left: Exceptional troubleshooting skills and advanced knowledge of internet technology
right: 5+ years of experience in customer support or technical support roles; management experience is a plus
growthAreas.synthesis: These don't cluster into one theme, but reflect that deep technical troubleshooting expertise is valuable background here, even though management experience is what's explicitly called out as needed.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from solving the hardest individual tickets to managing a team that handles many of them.

left: Fully autonomous; sets technical standards for support engineering
right: Balances team performance, coverage, and quality; navigates people situations, PIPs, and cross-team dynamics using established management frameworks
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect scheduling, coverage, and people-performance conversations to take up time that used to go entirely toward direct technical investigation.

mainTakeaways: This is a cross-family move from technical escalation work to team management, worth being clear-eyed that it's a track change, not a technical promotion.
Day-to-day, that plays out as trading deep individual troubleshooting for team coverage, coaching, and performance management.
Formal people-management experience is called out as a plus but not required, worth asking your manager what specific exposure would help you build confidence in that gap before making this move.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to informally mentor or onboard a Tier 1 or Tier 2 support agent to get a preview of the coaching and performance-monitoring side of this role.

analysis.headline: Moving from Support Operations Manager II to Customer Support Manager is a lateral move from a senior individual-contributor operations role to formal front-line team management.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Customer Support Manager JD calls for that the Support Operations Manager II JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Senior IC voice shaping support culture and strategy; cross-functional partner to Director and engineering on tooling and quality
right: Leads, manages, hires and trains offshore Tier 1 and Tier 2 support teams, providing guidance, performance monitoring, ongoing coaching and support
growthAreas.synthesis: Support Ops Manager II shapes culture and strategy as a senior IC, without direct reports; Customer Support Manager adds formal hiring and performance authority over Tier 1/2 agents, this is the actual move into people management.

left: Drives highest-complexity operational initiatives independently; shapes how support scales as a senior IC
right: Ensures strict adherence to customer service tier agreements (SLAs) and escalates critical issues as needed
growthAreas.synthesis: These don't cluster into one theme, but the underlying shift is from driving org-wide operational initiatives to being accountable for one team's day-to-day SLA performance.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from shaping support-wide operations to running one front-line team.

left: Fully autonomous as an IC; defines operational excellence standards across the support org
right: Balances team performance, coverage, and quality; navigates people situations, PIPs, and cross-team dynamics using established management frameworks
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect direct people management work like scheduling, coaching, and performance conversations to replace time previously spent on cross-org process and tooling initiatives.

mainTakeaways: This is a lateral move onto the people-management track rather than a step up in seniority, since Support Ops Manager II already carries significant scope as a senior IC.
Day-to-day, the real change is direct people management responsibility, which this role doesn't currently have as an IC.
Worth discussing with your manager whether you'd rather stay on the senior IC track (where your current scope already carries real influence) or move into direct people management specifically.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to shadow a Customer Support Manager's 1:1s and coaching conversations for a week before deciding this move is the right fit for you.

analysis.headline: Moving from Support Operations Manager II to Director of Customer Support is a 1-band jump from senior IC operations work to owning the entire global support function.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Director JD calls for that the Support Operations Manager II JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Senior IC voice shaping support culture and strategy; cross-functional partner to Director and engineering on tooling and quality
right: Leads and develops multiple support managers and their teams, building a strong culture of accountability and customer focus
growthAreas.synthesis: This is a bigger jump than it might look on paper: moving from an IC role (no direct reports) straight to managing multiple support managers, a manager-of-managers structure with no direct people-management step in between.

left: Drives highest-complexity operational initiatives independently; shapes how support scales as a senior IC
right: Sets support strategy, staffing plans, and operational goals aligned with company growth and customer needs
growthAreas.synthesis: Operational initiative-driving carries over conceptually, but the Director JD adds formal accountability for staffing plans and company-aligned goals, a different kind of ownership than executing initiatives as an IC.

left: 5+ years in customer support or technical support; people management experience strongly preferred
right: 7+ years of experience in technical support leadership, including managing managers
growthAreas.synthesis: These don't cluster into one theme, but reflect that the tenure and, critically, direct people-management experience bars both go up significantly at this level.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from senior IC operational work to running the whole support organization.

left: Fully autonomous as an IC; defines operational excellence standards across the support org
right: Leads and develops multiple support managers and their teams, building a strong culture of accountability and customer focus
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect regular time developing and coaching managers rather than doing the operational work yourself, a significant jump given there's no direct people-management step in between.

mainTakeaways: This is a notably large jump, moving straight from an IC role with no direct reports to managing managers, worth discussing an intermediate step with your manager if you haven't yet managed people directly.
Day-to-day, that gap shows up as a jump straight into developing and coaching managers, with no direct people-management experience to build on first.
If you're targeting this move, the Customer Support Manager role in between is worth exploring as a deliberate stepping stone rather than skipping it.

worthExploring.rec: Talk to your manager about whether a Customer Support Manager stint first would set you up better for managing managers later, since this JD assumes prior direct people-management experience.

analysis.headline: Moving from Marketing Innovation Manager to Director of Marketing is a 1-band jump from owning brand and content execution to leading the entire integrated marketing function.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Director of Marketing JD calls for that the Marketing Innovation Manager JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Shapes how Eleven shows up externally; key partner to VP of Sales and Product leadership
right: Leads and develops a team of marketing professionals across multiple disciplines
growthAreas.synthesis: The Marketing Innovation Manager JD explicitly has no direct reports (may oversee external consultants); the Director JD adds formal team leadership across multiple marketing disciplines, a genuine first step into people management here.

left: Balances strategic brand positioning with hands-on execution across events, content, and digital channels
right: Develops and executes an integrated marketing strategy spanning brand, demand generation, content, and product marketing
Owns marketing-sourced pipeline targets and reports on marketing performance against company goals
growthAreas.synthesis: Brand and content strategy carries over, but the Director JD adds formal pipeline targets and demand-generation ownership, tying marketing directly to revenue outcomes rather than brand presence alone.

left: 5+ years of experience in B2B SaaS marketing, ideally within hospitality tech or adjacent industries
right: 7+ years of experience in marketing leadership roles, preferably in B2B SaaS
growthAreas.synthesis: These don't cluster into one theme, but the underlying shift is tenure plus a track record specifically in marketing leadership, not just marketing execution.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from executing brand and content work to leading a team and owning pipeline outcomes.

left: Highly self-directed; manages budget, external consultants, and cross-functional campaigns
right: High autonomy; reports to VP of Sales; defines marketing OKRs and priorities
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect team management and OKR-setting to take up time that previously went toward hands-on content creation and event execution yourself.

mainTakeaways: The jump from Marketing Innovation Manager to Director of Marketing shows up most in Leadership & People Management, since the current role has no direct reports at all.
Day-to-day, that plays out as a shift from hands-on brand and content execution to leading a multi-discipline team and being accountable for pipeline numbers.
Since formal team leadership is new here, worth asking your VP for a stretch project managing an external consultant or contractor relationship as a first step.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to take on oversight of an external consultant or a specific campaign's contributors, since that's the closest current equivalent to managing a team.
Get involved in setting next quarter's marketing-sourced pipeline targets, even in a supporting role, to build fluency with that side of the Director JD.

analysis.headline: Moving from Product Marketing Manager to Director of Marketing is a 1-band jump from owning product positioning to leading the entire integrated marketing function.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Director of Marketing JD calls for that the Product Marketing Manager JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Bridges Product, Sales, and Marketing; shapes roadmap priorities through customer and market insights
right: Leads and develops a team of marketing professionals across multiple disciplines
growthAreas.synthesis: The Product Marketing Manager JD is individual-contributor and cross-functional by nature; the Director JD adds formal team leadership across disciplines the PMM role doesn't currently own, like brand and demand generation.

left: Leads go-to-market planning and execution for new product launches and feature releases
right: Develops and executes an integrated marketing strategy spanning brand, demand generation, content, and product marketing
Owns marketing-sourced pipeline targets and reports on marketing performance against company goals
growthAreas.synthesis: Go-to-market planning for product launches carries over, but the scope widens to owning strategy across all of marketing, plus formal pipeline accountability that product marketing alone doesn't carry.

left: 3+ years of experience in product marketing, preferably in B2B SaaS
right: 7+ years of experience in marketing leadership roles, preferably in B2B SaaS
Experience managing marketing budgets and measuring ROI across channels
growthAreas.synthesis: These are additional experience thresholds that don't cluster into one theme, mainly tenure and budget ownership across channels beyond just product marketing.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from product-focused messaging work to leading the full marketing function.

left: Conducts market and competitive research to inform positioning and strategy
right: Partners with Sales leadership to align marketing programs with sales priorities and target accounts
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect more time in cross-functional strategy alignment with Sales leadership and less time doing hands-on competitive research and messaging work yourself.

mainTakeaways: The jump from Product Marketing Manager to Director of Marketing shows up most in Leadership & People Management, since this JD is the first with formal team leadership.
Day-to-day, that plays out as owning strategy and team leadership across all of marketing, not just product positioning.
Since this expands well beyond product marketing into brand, demand gen, and events, it's worth building visibility into those other disciplines before targeting this move.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to co-lead a cross-discipline marketing initiative (spanning brand or demand gen, not just product marketing) to build credibility outside your current specialty.

analysis.headline: Moving from Technical Product Manager to Senior Product Manager is a 1-band jump from owning technical infrastructure areas to shaping strategic, customer-facing product direction.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Senior PM JD calls for that the Technical PM JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Represents technical product areas to internal stakeholders and, when needed, customers and partners
right: Serves as the chief sales engineer for assigned products as needed, demonstrating and selling the value of the ElevenOS platform
Establishes and maintains relationships with tech vendors and integration partners
growthAreas.synthesis: Customer contact goes from occasional, technical-context representation to an active sales-facing role, a notably more external and commercially-oriented responsibility.

left: Owns product requirements and roadmap for technical, infrastructure-level components of the platform
right: Establishes a product vision and roadmap that supports and aligns with the overall product strategy and vision
growthAreas.synthesis: Both roles own roadmap at a component level, but the Senior PM JD ties that explicitly to company-wide product strategy, a broader planning lens than infrastructure-level ownership.

left: Deep understanding of APIs, system architecture, and platform extensibility
right: 5+ years as a product manager for a complex product at an enterprise SaaS company, having launched multiple new products
Participates in industry workgroups and associations, providing thought leadership
growthAreas.synthesis: These don't cluster into one theme, but reflect a shift from deep technical/API expertise to a broader track record of shipping products and an external thought-leadership presence.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from technical infrastructure ownership to customer-facing strategic product work.

left: Partners deeply with Engineering on architecture, API design, and technical tradeoffs
right: Serves as the chief sales engineer for assigned products as needed
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect some weeks to include being pulled into live sales conversations to speak to product value, a new kind of external, commercially-focused work compared to engineering-facing technical tradeoffs.

mainTakeaways: The jump from Technical Product Manager to Senior Product Manager shows up most in Customer & External Relationships, moving from technical stakeholder representation to active sales-facing work.
Day-to-day, that plays out as more customer and sales-conversation time and comparatively less deep technical architecture involvement.

Since the Senior PM JD calls for a track record of multiple shipped products, worth mapping which of your technical infrastructure launches would count and which additional launches you'd want visibility into.

worthExploring.rec: Volunteer to join a customer or sales call to speak to the value of your product area directly, rather than only representing it in technical contexts.

analysis.headline: Moving from UX / Design Product Manager to Senior Product Manager is a 1-band jump from owning user experience for a product area to shaping company-wide product strategy.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the Senior PM JD calls for that the UX / Design PM JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Contributes to product strategy and shares knowledge
right: Establishes a product vision and roadmap that supports and aligns with the overall product strategy and vision
Gathers and prioritizes product and customer requirements, becoming an expert in the customer's jobs to be done
growthAreas.synthesis: The UX/Design PM JD contributes to strategy; the Senior PM JD owns setting it, a shift from providing design-informed input to being accountable for the roadmap and prioritization decisions themselves.

left: Works across functions to solve design challenges and seeks input from all relevant departments
right: Serves as the chief sales engineer for assigned products as needed, demonstrating and selling the value of the ElevenOS platform
Establishes and maintains relationships with tech vendors and integration partners
growthAreas.synthesis: Cross-functional collaboration carries over, but the Senior PM JD adds direct external customer and vendor relationship ownership, which the design-focused role doesn't call for.

left: Experience working with established UI component libraries, such as Material Design and Bootstrap
right: 5+ years as a product manager for a complex product at an enterprise SaaS company, having launched multiple new products
growthAreas.synthesis: These don't cluster into one theme, but reflect a shift from hands-on design tooling expertise to a broader product-management track record of shipped products.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from designing user experiences to owning product strategy and external relationships.

left: Produces development-ready visual mockups and prototypes (in Figma or similar)
right: Gathers and prioritizes product and customer requirements, becoming an expert in the customer's jobs to be done
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect less time in Figma producing mockups yourself and more time gathering and prioritizing requirements across a broader roadmap, a shift from design execution to strategic prioritization.

mainTakeaways: The jump from UX / Design Product Manager to Senior Product Manager shows up most in Strategy & Planning, moving from contributing to strategy to owning it.
Day-to-day, that plays out as less hands-on design work and more roadmap and requirements-prioritization time.
The external customer and vendor relationship expectations are new here, worth building deliberately if your current role is mostly internal-facing design work.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to own the roadmap prioritization for a specific feature area end to end, rather than just the design and prototyping portion of it.
Look for an opportunity to join a customer-facing conversation about your product area, since that's largely new at this level.

analysis.headline: Moving from Director of Data Operations to VP of Product is a 1-band jump from owning revenue-systems data infrastructure to leading the entire Product organization and strategy.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the VP of Product JD calls for that the Director of Data Operations JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Owns the HubSpot ecosystem and evaluates tooling and vendor decisions independently; no direct reports
right: Recruits, mentors, and scales a high-performing product team, fostering growth and development
Builds and leads a high-performing team of product managers, technical subject matter experts, product designers, operations managers, and documentation specialists
growthAreas.synthesis: This JD currently has no direct reports; the VP role adds full people-leadership responsibility across a large, multi-disciplinary team, a significant first step into formal management at real scale.

left: Designs and refines end to end dataflow processes across departments; resolves competing priorities between revenue generating teams
right: Defines and executes a product strategy that aligns with business goals, market trends, and customer needs
Develops and maintains a product roadmap that is transparent, prioritized, and outcome-driven
growthAreas.synthesis: Cross-department dataflow strategy carries over conceptually, but the VP JD extends that systems-thinking to the entire product portfolio and market strategy, a much broader strategic mandate.

left: 7+ years of experience in data engineering, data operations, or analytics leadership roles
right: 10+ years of experience in product management for enterprise SaaS, with at least 3 years in leadership roles
growthAreas.synthesis: These are additional experience thresholds that don't cluster into one theme, mainly a shift in domain from data operations leadership specifically to broader product management leadership.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from owning revenue-systems infrastructure to leading the whole product organization.

left: High autonomy; reports to the VP of Product with no direct reports
right: Fosters a culture of innovation, collaboration, and agility within the product organization
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect team leadership and culture-building work across a large, varied team to replace time previously spent on tooling, vendor, and dataflow decisions.

mainTakeaways: This is a notably large jump, given the current role has no direct reports and the VP role leads a large multi-disciplinary team, worth discussing an intermediate leadership step with your manager.
Day-to-day, that gap shows up as a jump straight into people leadership at scale, without a direct management step in between.
The domain shift from data operations to broad product management is also real, worth building visibility into product strategy and roadmap work specifically, not just cross-functional data systems.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to take on a direct report or lead a small cross-functional pod as a first step into people management before targeting a VP-level scope.
Get involved in product roadmap planning conversations specifically, since your current strategic work is more focused on data infrastructure than product direction.

analysis.headline: Moving from VP of Finance to CFO is a 1-band jump from owning the finance function operationally to setting company-wide financial strategy at the executive leadership table.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the CFO JD calls for that the VP of Finance JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Leads financial planning and analysis, budgeting, and forecasting processes for the company
right: Sets the company's overall financial strategy, including capital structure, budgeting, and long-term planning
growthAreas.synthesis: Budgeting and forecasting execution carries over directly, but the CFO JD adds capital structure decisions, a different order of strategic ownership over how the company is financed, not just how it plans.

left: Manages relationships with external auditors, banks, and financial partners
right: Leads fundraising, financing, and investor relations activities as needed
growthAreas.synthesis: External financial relationships carry over, but the CFO JD adds active fundraising and investor relations ownership, a more board- and investor-facing responsibility than banking and audit relationships alone.

left: 10+ years of progressive finance experience, including leadership roles
right: 12+ years of progressive finance leadership experience, including executive-level roles
growthAreas.synthesis: These are additional tenure and seniority thresholds that don't cluster into one theme, mainly a step up in required executive-level track record.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from running the finance function to setting company-wide financial direction with the board.

left: Presents financial results and analysis to the leadership team and board as needed
right: Serves as a key strategic partner to the CEO and board of directors on financial matters
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect board-facing conversations to shift from periodic reporting to ongoing strategic partnership, meaning the board relationship becomes a regular part of the role rather than an occasional presentation.

mainTakeaways: The jump from VP of Finance to CFO shows up most in Strategy & Planning, specifically capital structure and long-term financial strategy rather than budgeting execution.
Day-to-day, that plays out as a shift from periodic board reporting to ongoing strategic partnership with the CEO and board.
Fundraising and investor relations are explicit and new at this level, worth discussing with your CEO or board how much exposure you can get to those conversations before targeting this move.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to be more directly involved in a capital structure or financing decision, even in a supporting analytical role, to build direct exposure before it's your call to make.
Look for an opportunity to join an investor or board conversation directly, rather than through the CEO, since that relationship is central to the CFO role.

analysis.headline: Moving from DevOps Engineer to DevOps Manager is a 2-band skip-level jump, combining the technical growth of the Sr. DevOps Engineer step with a full move into team leadership.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the DevOps Manager JD calls for that the DevOps Engineer JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Limited application development in Python, Node, or TypeScript (troubleshoot a ticket, a few times a month)
Handles routine infrastructure issues and incidents; follows runbooks
right: Solves complex infrastructure challenges independently before this role even adds people leadership
Optimizes AWS environments to enhance developer flow, reduce risk, and increase visibility
growthAreas.synthesis: This pair skips the Sr. DevOps Engineer step, so the technical bar jumps twice at once: from following runbooks to proactively redesigning infrastructure, which is normally a step on its own before adding management.

left: Contributes within the DevOps team; collaborates with developers on deployment readiness
right: Leads and manages the DevOps team, ensuring effective collaboration and project delivery
Trains and mentors team members, especially within development and support teams
growthAreas.synthesis: There's no informal mentoring step here to build on first, this JD expects you to walk in already leading and coaching a team, not growing into it gradually.

left: 3+ years of experience as a DevOps Engineer, 2+ years using AWS
right: Strong leadership and management skills with experience leading a team
Documentation skills to thoroughly document processes and solutions
growthAreas.synthesis: These are additional hard and/or soft skills that don't cluster into one broader theme, mostly formal leadership experience layered directly on top of entry-level DevOps technical requirements.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts when skipping straight from routine infrastructure tasks to running a team.

left: Works with close guidance; escalates novel issues and infrastructure changes above a defined risk threshold
right: Leads and manages the DevOps team, ensuring effective collaboration and project delivery
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect a jump from working under guidance on defined tasks directly into running 1:1s, project delivery, and team coordination, with no intermediate step of proactive individual ownership first.

mainTakeaways: This is a genuine skip-level jump, not a typical next step, since it combines two normal promotions (to Sr. DevOps Engineer, then to Manager) into one.

Day-to-day, the biggest gap is the missing proactive-ownership step, this role assumes you're already comfortable driving infrastructure improvements independently before managing a team doing the same.
If you're targeting this move directly, it's worth discussing with your manager whether the Sr. DevOps Engineer step first would make you a stronger, more prepared candidate rather than skipping it.

worthExploring.rec: Take on a self-directed infrastructure improvement project now, without being asked, to demonstrate the proactive ownership this role assumes you already have.
Ask for an interim opportunity to informally lead a small project or mentor a newer engineer, since there's no built-in step for this in between your current role and this one.

analysis.headline: Moving from Sr. SecOps Engineer to IT Security & Infrastructure Manager is a jump from deep security specialization to owning security and IT infrastructure company-wide, including the helpdesk function.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the IT Security & Infrastructure Manager JD calls for that the Sr. SecOps Engineer JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Advises engineering on secure design patterns; trains team on threat modeling and security best practices
right: Manages a team responsible for keeping company systems secure, available, and performant
Oversees onboarding and offboarding of staff and contractors, including computers and software applications
growthAreas.synthesis: Training and advising engineering carries over, but the Manager JD adds formal team management plus IT helpdesk and onboarding/offboarding oversight, a much broader operational scope than pure security engineering.

left: Proactively manages and improves security Key Lifecycle Objectives (KLOs) such as vulnerability scanning and certificate management
right: Implements and manages security compliance frameworks (e.g., ISO 27001, SOC 2, GDPR, CCPA, PCI-DSS)
Responds to security and privacy inquiries from customers and potential customers in support of the sales team
growthAreas.synthesis: Both roles touch compliance, but the Manager JD adds customer-facing compliance conversations directly supporting Sales, a new external, business-facing dimension to security work.

left: Deep experience with AWS security services: IAM, WAF, Config, Inspector, Security Hub, and KMS
right: Proficiency in managing firewalls, IDS/IPS, VPNs, and endpoint security solutions
7+ years of experience in IT security, infrastructure, DevOps, management, or related fields
growthAreas.synthesis: These are additional hard and/or soft skills that don't cluster into one broader theme, mainly broader IT infrastructure and endpoint security exposure beyond AWS-specific security depth.

analysis.dayToDaySub: How the daily work shifts from deep security engineering to running security, infrastructure, and IT support together.

left: Largely self-directed; defines security standards and tooling choices within their domain
right: Manages and monitors network security, firewalls, endpoint protection, and cloud security measures
Oversees IT Helpdesk activities to ensure timely response and resolution of requests
dayToDay.synthesis: Day-to-day, expect IT helpdesk and general infrastructure operations to become part of your responsibility alongside security, a broader operational plate than pure security work.

mainTakeaways: The jump from Sr. SecOps Engineer to IT Security & Infrastructure Manager shows up most in Leadership & People Management, this role manages people and IT operations, not just security.
Day-to-day, that plays out as taking on IT helpdesk and general infrastructure oversight in addition to your existing security depth.
Since this role touches customer-facing compliance conversations for Sales, worth asking your manager for exposure to a security or compliance customer inquiry to build that muscle.

worthExploring.rec: Ask to shadow or take on oversight of a smaller operational area, like onboarding/offboarding or helpdesk escalations, to build comfort outside pure security engineering.
Volunteer to help respond to a customer security questionnaire alongside Sales, since that's a new customer-facing dimension at this level.

analysis.headline: Moving from QA Engineer to QA Manager is a skip-level jump from hands-on lab and hardware testing to leading the company's overall test strategy and team.
analysis.growthAreasSub: What the QA Manager JD calls for that the QA Engineer JD doesn't, grounded in both job descriptions.

left: Contributes quality feedback within the sprint team; develops relationships with developers and product stakeholders
right: Coordinates and collaborates with BA, Dev, DevOps, and Test teams effectively to deliver story points on time
Is responsible for overall backend/API and frontend product quality
growthAreas.synthesis: Sprint-team quality feedback carries over, but the Manager JD adds accountability for overall product quality across teams, a company-wide ownership rather than a single sprint team's contribution.

left: Designs and manages virtual labs to test network services with a variety of operating systems and hardware
right: Implements structured testing frameworks (e.g., Cucumber) and autotests
Previous WebUI automation experience with Selenium/Cypress or other automation tools
growthAreas.synthesis: This is a notable domain shift: the QA Engineer JD is focused on network hardware lab testing, while the Manager JD is centered on WebUI and API test automation frameworks, a different technical specialty entirely.

left: At least 3 years of experience with networking and virtualization
right: ISTQB Certified Foundation Level Tester
